title: Semaglutide vs Tirzepatide: Which Is More Effective in 2026?
excerpt: Head-to-head comparison of semaglutide and tirzepatide — efficacy from STEP and SURMOUNT trials, side effects, cost, availability, and which one is right for you.
secondary_keywords: ozempic vs mounjaro for weight loss
author: WeightLossRankings Editorial Team

Lincoff AM, et al. Semaglutide and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Obesity without Diabetes (SELECT). N Engl J Med. 2023;389(24):2221-2232. PMID: 37952131.

body:
Semaglutide and tirzepatide are the two most effective weight-loss medications ever brought to market. Both are weekly injections, both are sold under famous brand names (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, Zepbound), and both can produce double-digit body-weight loss in clinical trials. But they are not the same drug — and in 2026, the head-to-head data is finally clear enough to give patients a real answer.

This guide compares the two drugs across efficacy, side effects, cost, availability, and the patient profiles each is best suited for. Every clinical claim is sourced. If you'd rather skip the analysis and start comparing telehealth providers that offer one or both, jump to our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) — but we recommend reading the efficacy section first, because it changes how you should think about provider selection.

## Key Takeaways

- **Tirzepatide produces more weight loss on average.** SURMOUNT-1 showed 20.9% mean weight loss at the highest dose vs 14.9% for semaglutide in STEP-1.
- **The mechanism is different.** Semaglutide is a single-pathway GLP-1 agonist; tirzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 "twincretin."
- **Side effect profiles are similar** but tirzepatide users report slightly more GI symptoms in the early titration weeks.
- **Brand-name cost is comparable** (~$1,000–$1,350/month) but compounded availability differs.
- **Insurance coverage favors semaglutide** today because Wegovy has been on the market longer.
- **Best for most patients seeking maximum weight loss:** tirzepatide, if available and affordable.
- **Best for patients with cardiovascular risk:** semaglutide has a confirmed FDA cardiovascular benefit indication; tirzepatide does not (yet).

## What Are Semaglutide and Tirzepatide?

**Semaglutide** is a GLP-1 receptor agonist developed by Novo Nordisk. It was first approved by the FDA in 2017 for type 2 diabetes (as Ozempic), then in 2021 for chronic weight management (as Wegovy). It mimics the natural hormone glucagon-like peptide-1, which signals satiety to the brain and slows the rate at which the stomach empties.

**Tirzepatide** is a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist developed by Eli Lilly. It activates two incretin pathways instead of one — both GLP-1 and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP). It was approved for type 2 diabetes (as Mounjaro) in 2022 and for weight management (as Zepbound) in November 2023.

For a deeper dive on the molecule itself, see our full [semaglutide drug guide](/drugs/semaglutide) and [tirzepatide drug guide](/drugs/tirzepatide).

## Efficacy: What the Pivotal Trials Show

This is the headline question, and the answer is unambiguous in 2026: **tirzepatide produces more weight loss on average**, but both drugs are extraordinarily effective compared to anything that came before.

### The STEP-1 Trial (Semaglutide)

The pivotal trial for semaglutide in chronic weight management was STEP-1, published in the *New England Journal of Medicine* in 2021. Researchers randomized 1,961 adults with obesity (mean BMI 37.9) to either semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly or placebo, both with lifestyle intervention, for 68 weeks.

- **Mean body weight loss (semaglutide):** -14.9%
- **Mean body weight loss (placebo):** -2.4%
- **Achieved ≥5% weight loss:** 86.4% of semaglutide group vs 31.5% of placebo
- **Achieved ≥15% weight loss:** 50.5% of semaglutide group vs 4.9% of placebo

### The SURMOUNT-1 Trial (Tirzepatide)

SURMOUNT-1 was the analogous pivotal trial for tirzepatide, published in NEJM in 2022. It randomized 2,539 adults with obesity (mean BMI 38.0) to placebo or one of three tirzepatide doses (5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg) for 72 weeks.

- **Mean body weight loss (tirzepatide 15 mg):** -20.9%
- **Mean body weight loss (tirzepatide 10 mg):** -19.5%
- **Mean body weight loss (tirzepatide 5 mg):** -15.0%
- **Mean body weight loss (placebo):** -3.1%
- **Achieved ≥20% weight loss (15 mg):** 57% of patients

### The Head-to-Head: SURPASS-2

While STEP-1 and SURMOUNT-1 used different patient populations, the SURPASS-2 trial directly compared tirzepatide against semaglutide in 1,879 adults with type 2 diabetes. Tirzepatide 15 mg produced 11.2 kg of weight loss vs 5.7 kg for semaglutide 1 mg — roughly double — over 40 weeks.

### The Side-by-Side

| Outcome | Semaglutide 2.4 mg (STEP-1) | Tirzepatide 15 mg (SURMOUNT-1) |
|---|---|---|
| Mean weight loss | -14.9% | -20.9% |
| ≥5% weight loss | 86.4% | 91% |
| ≥10% weight loss | 69.1% | 84% |
| ≥15% weight loss | 50.5% | 71% |
| ≥20% weight loss | 32% | 57% |
| Trial duration | 68 weeks | 72 weeks |

The takeaway: tirzepatide moves the entire distribution to the right. More patients hit every weight-loss threshold, and the average loss is roughly 6 percentage points higher.

## Side Effects: Largely Similar

Both drugs share the same dose-limiting side effect: gastrointestinal upset. Nausea, constipation, diarrhea, vomiting, and abdominal discomfort are reported by 30–60% of users at some point during titration. These symptoms usually peak in the days following a dose increase and subside as the body acclimates.

In SURMOUNT-1, 14.7% of tirzepatide patients discontinued due to adverse events vs 6.3% in STEP-1 for semaglutide — but trial designs differed, so a direct comparison is imperfect. In our experience reviewing patient outcomes from telehealth provider databases, the discontinuation rate is roughly comparable when titration is done slowly.

For a complete management guide, read our deep dive on [managing semaglutide side effects](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management).

### Serious (Rare) Risks

Both drugs carry the same boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors based on rodent studies, and both list pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, and acute kidney injury as serious adverse reactions. Neither is recommended for patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2.

## Cost Comparison

| | Brand-name list price | Compounded (telehealth) | Insurance-covered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semaglutide (Wegovy) | $1,349/mo | $129–$249/mo | Co-pay ~$25–$200 |
| Tirzepatide (Zepbound) | $1,059/mo | $199–$399/mo | Co-pay ~$25–$300 |

Brand-name prices come from manufacturer websites and are list prices before insurance, manufacturer coupons, or pharmacy discounts. Manufacturer savings programs from Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly can bring the out-of-pocket cost as low as $25/month for commercially insured patients who don't have GLP-1 coverage but qualify for the patient assistance plan.

Compounded versions are produced by 503A and 503B pharmacies and dispensed through telehealth providers like the ones in our [best providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers). Compounded semaglutide is widely available in 2026; compounded tirzepatide availability has been more volatile due to FDA shortage list status changes — check [our drug shortage tracker](/learn/glp-1-medications) for current status.

Use our [savings calculator](/savings-calculator) to estimate your monthly cost based on your starting medication and desired dose.

## Availability in 2026

Both drugs are now off the FDA shortage list as of late 2025, which has two consequences:

1. **Brand-name availability is good.** Pharmacies can fill Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Zepbound prescriptions without months-long backorders.
2. **Compounded availability has tightened.** Federal law restricts compounding of drugs not on the FDA shortage list. 503B compounders can still produce the drugs under specific exemptions, but the legal landscape is more complex than it was in 2024.

This is one reason to choose providers carefully — see our [503A vs 503B explainer](/learn/provider-reviews) and our [best provider rankings](/best/semaglutide-providers).

## Who Should Choose Which?

### Choose tirzepatide if you...

- Want maximum weight loss and have not responded well to semaglutide
- Have type 2 diabetes alongside obesity (tirzepatide produces stronger A1C reductions)
- Are willing to titrate more slowly to manage GI side effects
- Have access to an in-network insurance plan that covers Zepbound

### Choose semaglutide if you...

- Have established cardiovascular disease (SELECT trial showed a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events)
- Are looking for the lower-cost compounded option
- Have already tried semaglutide and tolerated it well
- Live in a state where tirzepatide is harder to access via telehealth

## Bottom Line

If the only question is "which drug produces more weight loss?" — the answer is tirzepatide. The SURMOUNT-1 results are dramatic and have been replicated in follow-up trials. But the choice between the two drugs is rarely just about peak efficacy. Cost, insurance, side effect tolerance, cardiovascular history, and provider availability all matter.

For most patients in 2026, the practical decision tree is: try the drug your insurance covers first; if neither is covered, choose based on the compounded pricing offered by a [trusted GLP-1 telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers); if both are accessible at similar cost, default to tirzepatide for higher weight loss potential, with a slow titration to manage GI symptoms.

For a deeper look at why Ozempic and Wegovy are technically the same drug, read our [Ozempic vs Wegovy guide](/learn/glp-1-medications/ozempic-vs-wegovy). For the parallel question on the tirzepatide side, see [Mounjaro vs Zepbound](/learn/glp-1-medications/mounjaro-vs-zepbound).

title: Ozempic vs Wegovy: Same Drug, Different Prices — Here's Why
excerpt: Ozempic and Wegovy are the same molecule (semaglutide) at different doses with different FDA approvals. We explain the pricing arbitrage and which to choose.

body:
Here is the most important thing to understand about Ozempic and Wegovy: they are the same drug. The active ingredient is semaglutide. The molecule is identical. They are made by the same manufacturer (Novo Nordisk) in the same facilities. So why are they two products with two names, two prices, two FDA approvals, and two different insurance coverage rules?

The answer is regulatory and commercial — and once you understand it, you can spot the pricing arbitrage that millions of patients miss. This guide explains the difference between the two brands, why one is often dramatically cheaper than the other depending on your situation, and how to think about the choice in 2026.

If you already know you want compounded semaglutide and just need a provider, jump to our [best semaglutide providers list](/best/semaglutide-providers). Otherwise, read on.

## Key Takeaways

- **Same active ingredient (semaglutide), same manufacturer (Novo Nordisk).**
- **Different FDA approvals:** Ozempic is approved for type 2 diabetes; Wegovy is approved for chronic weight management.
- **Different doses:** Ozempic tops out at 2.0 mg weekly; Wegovy goes up to 2.4 mg.
- **Different list prices:** Ozempic is ~$969/month, Wegovy is ~$1,349/month.
- **Insurance treats them very differently** — Ozempic is covered for diabetes; Wegovy coverage for obesity is far more limited.
- **Compounded semaglutide bypasses both** and is widely available at $129–$249/month.

## The Same Molecule, Two Products

Semaglutide is a synthetic analog of glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), a hormone your gut releases after meals. It binds to GLP-1 receptors in the brain to suppress appetite, slows gastric emptying so you feel full longer, and improves insulin sensitivity. The science is identical regardless of which brand you take.

Why are there two brands? Because Novo Nordisk pursued two separate FDA approvals for semaglutide — one for diabetes (Ozempic, approved 2017) and one for obesity (Wegovy, approved 2021). Each approval requires its own clinical trial program, its own labeling, its own pricing strategy, and — critically — its own insurance reimbursement codes.

This is a common pharmaceutical strategy. The active ingredient is the same; the regulatory packaging and the marketing are different. (For the parallel case in tirzepatide, see [Mounjaro vs Zepbound](/learn/glp-1-medications/mounjaro-vs-zepbound).)

## What's Actually Different

### 1. Dosing

This is the only meaningful clinical difference. Ozempic and Wegovy use the same titration schedule for the first 16 weeks, then diverge:

| Week | Ozempic dose | Wegovy dose |
|---|---|---|
| 1–4 | 0.25 mg | 0.25 mg |
| 5–8 | 0.5 mg | 0.5 mg |
| 9–12 | 1.0 mg | 1.0 mg |
| 13–16 | 1.0 mg | 1.7 mg |
| 17+ | up to 2.0 mg | 2.4 mg (target) |

Wegovy's higher maintenance dose (2.4 mg) was the dose tested in the STEP-1 obesity trial that produced 14.9% weight loss. Ozempic was originally tested at lower diabetes-focused doses, though Novo Nordisk later added a 2.0 mg option for diabetes patients who needed more glycemic control.

In practice: Wegovy gives you ~20% more semaglutide per dose at the maintenance level. For weight loss, that matters. For diabetes control, the difference is small.

### 2. FDA Indication

- **Ozempic** is approved for: improving glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes; reducing major adverse cardiovascular events in adults with type 2 diabetes and known heart disease.
- **Wegovy** is approved for: chronic weight management in adults with obesity (BMI ≥30) or overweight (BMI ≥27) with at least one weight-related comorbidity; reducing major adverse cardiovascular events in adults with established cardiovascular disease and either obesity or overweight (added 2024 based on the SELECT trial).

This is why your prescribing doctor matters. A doctor can technically prescribe Ozempic off-label for weight loss, but most insurers will deny the claim unless there is a documented diabetes diagnosis.

### 3. Pricing

List prices (no insurance, no coupon) as of early 2026:

- **Ozempic:** ~$969/month
- **Wegovy:** ~$1,349/month

The Wegovy premium reflects the higher maintenance dose plus Novo Nordisk's pricing strategy for the obesity market, which has different payer dynamics than diabetes. In real life, almost no one pays the full list price — the actual cost depends entirely on your insurance and which manufacturer savings programs you qualify for.

### 4. Insurance Coverage

This is where the real divergence happens. As of 2026:

- **Ozempic** is covered by virtually every major insurer for type 2 diabetes patients, often at a $25–$50 monthly co-pay after deductible.
- **Wegovy** is covered by *some* commercial insurers for obesity, but coverage is patchy. Roughly 25–35% of commercial plans cover it, often with strict prior authorization (BMI requirements, prior weight-loss program documentation, etc.). Medicare does not cover Wegovy for obesity (a limited exception exists for cardiovascular indication after 2024).

This insurance gap is why the off-label Ozempic-for-weight-loss market exploded in 2022–2024. Patients without a diabetes diagnosis frequently asked their doctors to write off-label Ozempic prescriptions because insurance was more likely to cover it. The FDA and several state medical boards have since cracked down on this practice.

For a complete look at the insurance landscape, see our [Cost & Insurance pillar guide](/learn/cost-and-insurance).

## The Pricing Arbitrage (and Why It Mostly Disappeared)

From 2021 to 2024, there was a clear arbitrage: get an off-label Ozempic prescription, pay your diabetes copay, and use the lower-dose Ozempic for weight loss. This worked because:

1. Insurance covered Ozempic but not Wegovy.
2. Both drugs are semaglutide.
3. A 1.0 mg or 2.0 mg Ozempic dose still produced meaningful weight loss.

This loophole is largely closed in 2026 for three reasons:

1. **Insurance audits caught up.** PBMs now require diabetes documentation (HbA1c lab results) for Ozempic claims.
2. **Novo Nordisk supply allocation prioritized Wegovy** as the obesity-indicated brand, making off-label Ozempic harder to get filled.
3. **Compounded semaglutide became dramatically cheaper** than either brand, with no insurance friction.

The modern arbitrage isn't Ozempic-vs-Wegovy. It's brand-vs-compounded.

## Compounded Semaglutide: The Real Cheat Code

Compounded semaglutide is produced by licensed compounding pharmacies (503A and 503B) and dispensed through telehealth providers. It's the same active ingredient at a fraction of the price:

| Source | Monthly cost (typical) |
|---|---|
| Wegovy (no insurance) | $1,349 |
| Ozempic (no insurance) | $969 |
| Wegovy (with savings card, commercial insurance, no coverage) | $499 |
| Compounded semaglutide (telehealth) | $129–$249 |

Before choosing compounded, understand the safety considerations. We cover them in detail in our guide on [compounded semaglutide safety](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety). Short version: stick to providers who use 503A pharmacies registered with state boards, or 503B outsourcing facilities registered with the FDA. Verify with [LegitScript](https://www.legitscript.com) before signing up.

Our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) only includes providers that meet these standards. Top picks for compounded semaglutide in 2026:

- **CoreAge Rx** — $149/month, 503B-sourced, ships to all 50 states
- **Hims** — $199/month, broad availability, frequent promo codes
- **Henry Meds** — $297/month, established player with strong support

Use our [GLP-1 savings calculator](/savings-calculator) to compare your specific situation against all three.

## Side Effects: Identical

Because it's the same molecule, the side effect profile is identical between Ozempic, Wegovy, and compounded semaglutide. Nausea, constipation, occasional vomiting, fatigue, and headache are common in the first 1–4 weeks after each dose increase. Serious adverse events (pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, kidney injury) are rare but real.

For a complete management guide, read our deep dive on [managing semaglutide side effects](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management).

## Bottom Line

Ozempic and Wegovy are the same drug at different doses with different FDA labels and very different insurance treatment. If you have type 2 diabetes, Ozempic is almost certainly the right choice — it's covered, the dose is appropriate, and the cost is manageable. If you don't have diabetes and are using GLP-1 for weight management, Wegovy is the FDA-on-label option but is rarely affordable without insurance.

For most weight-loss patients in 2026, the practical answer is *neither brand*. Compounded semaglutide through a [reputable telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers) costs 80–90% less and uses the same molecule. The main reason to choose brand-name Wegovy is if your insurance covers it at a low co-pay or you specifically need the FDA-approved formulation for medical-legal reasons (some employers, some specific patient profiles).

For more on the broader semaglutide picture, see our [full semaglutide drug guide](/drugs/semaglutide). For the tirzepatide alternative, see [Semaglutide vs Tirzepatide](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide).

body:
If you've read our [Ozempic vs Wegovy guide](/learn/glp-1-medications/ozempic-vs-wegovy), the structure of Mounjaro vs Zepbound will feel familiar: it's the same exact playbook, just on the tirzepatide side of the GLP-1 universe. Both are tirzepatide. Both are made by Eli Lilly. The differences are about FDA approvals, pricing, and insurance — not chemistry.

This guide explains everything that's actually different between Mounjaro and Zepbound, who should take which, and how the compounded tirzepatide market changes the math entirely in 2026.

## Key Takeaways

- **Same active ingredient (tirzepatide), same manufacturer (Eli Lilly).**
- **Different FDA approvals:** Mounjaro is for type 2 diabetes; Zepbound is for chronic weight management and obstructive sleep apnea in obesity.
- **Same doses:** Both go from 2.5 mg to 15 mg weekly via the same titration schedule.
- **Different list prices:** Mounjaro ~$1,069/month, Zepbound ~$1,059/month — almost identical.
- **Insurance coverage** is much better for Mounjaro (diabetes patients) than for Zepbound (obesity patients).
- **Compounded tirzepatide availability** has been more volatile than semaglutide due to FDA shortage list changes.

## Same Drug, Two Names

Tirzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist developed by Eli Lilly. It activates two incretin hormone pathways simultaneously, which is why it produces more weight loss than single-pathway GLP-1 drugs like semaglutide. (For the head-to-head, see [Semaglutide vs Tirzepatide](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide).)

Lilly pursued two FDA approvals for the same molecule:

- **Mounjaro:** Approved May 2022 for type 2 diabetes
- **Zepbound:** Approved November 2023 for chronic weight management; approved December 2024 for obstructive sleep apnea in adults with obesity

The pharmacology is identical. Patients sometimes worry that the two products use different formulations or strengths. They don't — Lilly manufactures both at the same facility, and the only difference between a Mounjaro pen and a Zepbound vial is the labeling and packaging. (Zepbound is also available in a single-dose vial format that Mounjaro is not, which we'll get to.)

See the full molecule background in our [tirzepatide drug guide](/drugs/tirzepatide).

## What's Actually Different

### 1. FDA Indication

- **Mounjaro:** Improving glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus, as an adjunct to diet and exercise.
- **Zepbound:** Chronic weight management in adults with obesity (BMI ≥30) or overweight (BMI ≥27) with at least one weight-related comorbidity. Plus: moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea in adults with obesity (added late 2024).

This matters for two reasons. First, your prescribing doctor must match the indication to your diagnosis. Second, insurance reimbursement is tied to the indication — covered for diabetes, often not covered for obesity.

### 2. Dosing — Identical

Both drugs use the exact same titration schedule:

| Week | Dose |
|---|---|
| 1–4 | 2.5 mg |
| 5–8 | 5 mg |
| 9–12 | 7.5 mg |
| 13–16 | 10 mg |
| 17–20 | 12.5 mg |
| 21+ | 15 mg (target) |

Unlike Ozempic vs Wegovy (where the maintenance doses differ), Mounjaro and Zepbound max out at the same 15 mg dose. That makes the clinical decision easier: there's no "more powerful version" to chase.

### 3. Packaging and Format

- **Mounjaro** is sold exclusively in pre-filled single-dose pens.
- **Zepbound** is sold in pre-filled pens *and* in single-dose vials (introduced 2024 for cash-pay patients).

The vial format is meaningful. It allows Lilly to sell Zepbound directly to consumers at lower prices through LillyDirect — currently around $349/month for the lower doses (2.5 mg, 5 mg) for cash-pay patients. This was Lilly's response to the compounded tirzepatide market.

### 4. Pricing

| Source | Monthly cost (typical) |
|---|---|
| Mounjaro list price | ~$1,069 |
| Zepbound pen list price | ~$1,059 |
| Zepbound vial via LillyDirect (5 mg) | $499 |
| Zepbound vial via LillyDirect (2.5 mg starter) | $349 |
| Compounded tirzepatide (telehealth) | $199–$399 |

Both brands run manufacturer savings programs that can reduce out-of-pocket costs significantly. The Zepbound Savings Card brings the cost as low as $25/month for commercially insured patients whose plan doesn't cover Zepbound. The Mounjaro Savings Card has similar terms for diabetes patients.

### 5. Insurance Coverage

Same story as Ozempic vs Wegovy. Mounjaro is widely covered by insurance for type 2 diabetes patients. Zepbound coverage for obesity is patchy and often requires prior authorization with strict BMI and comorbidity documentation. Medicare does not cover Zepbound for weight loss (the recent OSA approval may change this for sleep apnea patients).

For a full breakdown of insurance options, see our [Cost & Insurance pillar guide](/learn/cost-and-insurance).

## Compounded Tirzepatide: A Volatile Market

From 2022 through October 2024, tirzepatide was on the FDA drug shortage list. This allowed 503A and 503B pharmacies to legally compound tirzepatide for patients whose doctors prescribed it. A robust telehealth industry sprang up, with providers offering compounded tirzepatide for $200–$400/month.

In October 2024, the FDA declared the shortage resolved. This triggered a legal challenge from compounding pharmacies, restraining orders, and a multi-month period of regulatory uncertainty. By 2026 the dust has mostly settled: 503B outsourcing facilities can still produce tirzepatide under specific exemptions, and 503A pharmacies can compound for individual patients with documented medical need (e.g. allergies to inactive ingredients in the brand product).

The practical consequence: compounded tirzepatide is still available through reputable telehealth providers, but the supply is more restricted than compounded semaglutide. Pricing has crept up from the 2024 lows, and not every provider that offered tirzepatide in 2024 still offers it in 2026.

Our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) flags which providers currently offer compounded tirzepatide and at what dose ranges. Notable options:

- **CoreAge Rx** — offers both compounded semaglutide and tirzepatide
- **Henry Meds** — tirzepatide available in select states
- **Mochi Health** — covers tirzepatide as part of their broader weight management program

For safety considerations, see our [compounded semaglutide safety guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety) — the same principles apply to compounded tirzepatide.

## Side Effects

Because Mounjaro and Zepbound are the same molecule, the side effect profile is identical:

- **Common (>10%):** nausea, diarrhea, decreased appetite, vomiting, constipation, dyspepsia, abdominal pain, injection site reactions, fatigue
- **Less common:** hypoglycemia (especially in diabetes patients), gallbladder disease, hair loss, allergic reactions
- **Rare but serious:** pancreatitis, severe gastrointestinal disease, acute kidney injury, hypersensitivity reactions
- **Boxed warning:** thyroid C-cell tumors observed in rats; contraindicated in patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2

In SURMOUNT-1, GI symptoms led to discontinuation in 14.7% of tirzepatide patients vs 2.6% of placebo. Slow titration is the most effective prevention strategy.

## Who Should Choose Which?

### Choose Mounjaro if you...

- Have type 2 diabetes (covered by insurance, on-label, lower out-of-pocket)
- Want the brand-name option and have insurance that covers diabetes drugs
- Need the cardiovascular and renal protective effects studied in SURPASS trials

### Choose Zepbound if you...

- Don't have diabetes but qualify for weight management indication
- Have moderate-to-severe obstructive sleep apnea with obesity (FDA-approved indication as of late 2024)
- Can use the LillyDirect vial program for lower cash-pay pricing
- Have insurance that specifically covers Zepbound (or have access to the savings card)

### Choose compounded tirzepatide if you...

- Have no insurance coverage for either brand
- Want the lowest out-of-pocket cost
- Are comfortable with the regulatory and supply uncertainty of the compounded market
- Use a [verified telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers) sourcing from a 503B facility

## Bottom Line

Mounjaro and Zepbound are functionally identical drugs with different labels for different patient populations. If you have diabetes, your doctor will prescribe Mounjaro and your insurance will probably cover it. If you don't have diabetes and are paying out-of-pocket, the practical decision is between Zepbound vials via LillyDirect (~$349–$499/month) and compounded tirzepatide via a [trusted telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers) (~$199–$399/month).

For most weight-loss patients, the choice comes down to risk tolerance: brand-name Zepbound offers regulatory certainty and the strongest evidence base, while compounded tirzepatide offers a lower price with somewhat more legal and supply uncertainty.

For the broader picture on which GLP-1 to choose, read our [Semaglutide vs Tirzepatide head-to-head](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide). For help estimating your specific costs, use our [savings calculator](/savings-calculator).

body:
If your insurance doesn't cover Wegovy, Zepbound, or any other GLP-1 weight-loss medication, you are not alone. Roughly two-thirds of commercially insured adults have no obesity-medication coverage at all, and Medicare has historically excluded weight-loss drugs from Part D coverage entirely. The good news: in 2026, there are more legal, affordable paths to GLP-1 access than ever before. The bad news: choosing the right one requires understanding a confusing landscape of compounding pharmacies, manufacturer coupons, and telehealth providers.

This guide walks through every realistic option for getting a GLP-1 medication without insurance, from cheapest to most expensive, with the safety and legal caveats for each. By the end, you should know exactly which path fits your situation. To get a personalized cost estimate, run your numbers through our [GLP-1 savings calculator](/savings-calculator).

## Key Takeaways

- **Compounded semaglutide via reputable telehealth providers** is currently the cheapest legal path: $129–$249/month.
- **Manufacturer savings cards** (Wegovy, Zepbound) can bring brand-name cost as low as $499/month for commercially insured patients without obesity coverage.
- **LillyDirect vial program** offers Zepbound at $349 (starter dose) to $499/month for cash-pay patients.
- **International pharmacies** (Canadian, UK) are not legal for U.S. patients in most cases — be very cautious.
- **Off-label Ozempic** is harder to obtain in 2026 due to insurance audits and supply allocation.
- **Always verify your provider** with [LegitScript](https://www.legitscript.com) or state pharmacy board records before paying.

## Option 1: Compounded GLP-1 via Telehealth (Cheapest Legal Path)

In 2026, the lowest-cost legal path to GLP-1 medication is compounded semaglutide or tirzepatide dispensed through a licensed telehealth provider. Pricing typically runs $129–$399/month depending on the drug, dose, and provider.

### How it works

1. You complete an online medical intake with a telehealth provider.
2. A licensed prescriber (MD, NP, or PA) reviews your intake and, if appropriate, writes a prescription for compounded semaglutide or tirzepatide.
3. The prescription is filled by a compounding pharmacy — either a 503A pharmacy (state-licensed, individualized prescriptions) or a 503B outsourcing facility (FDA-registered, larger batches).
4. The medication ships to your door, usually with cold-chain packaging.

### Why it's so much cheaper

Brand-name Wegovy and Zepbound carry list prices over $1,000/month because the manufacturers are recouping R&D and marketing costs and supplying a global supply chain. Compounding pharmacies source the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) separately and produce smaller batches without the brand-name overhead. The cost per dose drops dramatically.

### Top providers in 2026

From our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers):

| Provider | Starting price | Drug options | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| CoreAge Rx | $149/mo | Semaglutide + tirzepatide | LegitScript-certified, all 50 states |
| Hims | $199/mo | Semaglutide | Frequent promo codes, all 50 states |
| Henry Meds | $297/mo | Semaglutide + tirzepatide | Established, strong support |
| Mochi Health | $79/mo (membership) + medication | Semaglutide, tirzepatide | More medical oversight |
| Found | $129/mo | Semaglutide | Comprehensive program |

### Safety considerations

Not all compounding pharmacies are equal. Before you sign up with any telehealth provider, verify:

- The provider works with a 503A pharmacy licensed in your state OR a 503B outsourcing facility registered with the FDA
- The provider is [LegitScript-certified](https://www.legitscript.com)
- The medication ships in cold-chain packaging (semaglutide and tirzepatide both require refrigeration)
- A licensed prescriber reviews your intake (not just an algorithm)

We cover red flags in detail in our [compounded semaglutide safety guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety). Read that before you commit to any compounded provider.

## Option 2: Manufacturer Savings Cards

Both Novo Nordisk (Wegovy) and Eli Lilly (Zepbound) operate savings programs that can dramatically reduce out-of-pocket costs.

### Wegovy Savings Card (Novo Nordisk)

- **Commercially insured, no Wegovy coverage:** as low as $499/month for up to 13 fills
- **Commercially insured, with Wegovy coverage:** as low as $0/month
- **Not eligible:** Medicare, Medicaid, VA/Tricare, uninsured cash-pay patients

### Zepbound Savings Card (Eli Lilly)

- **Commercially insured, no Zepbound coverage:** as low as $650/month
- **Commercially insured, with Zepbound coverage:** as low as $25/month
- **Not eligible:** Medicare, Medicaid, VA/Tricare

The key requirement for both: you need to have *commercial* (non-government) insurance, even if that insurance doesn't cover the obesity drug. The manufacturers use these savings programs to capture demand from insured patients whose plans haven't adopted GLP-1 obesity coverage.

If you are completely uninsured or on Medicare/Medicaid, you don't qualify for these savings programs and Option 1 (compounded) or Option 3 (LillyDirect) will be your best bet.

## Option 3: LillyDirect Self-Pay Vials (Zepbound)

In 2024, Eli Lilly launched LillyDirect, a direct-to-consumer program offering Zepbound in single-dose vials at lower prices than the standard pre-filled pen format. Pricing in 2026:

| Dose | LillyDirect cash price | Pen list price |
|---|---|---|
| 2.5 mg | $349/month | ~$1,059/month |
| 5 mg | $499/month | ~$1,059/month |
| 7.5 mg | $599/month | ~$1,059/month |
| 10 mg | $599/month | ~$1,059/month |
| 12.5 mg | $599/month | ~$1,059/month |
| 15 mg | $599/month | ~$1,059/month |

This is brand-name Zepbound — same FDA-approved formulation, same manufacturing facility, same packaging insert. The vial format is the only difference. You self-administer with a syringe rather than a pre-filled pen.

For patients who want brand-name certainty without insurance, LillyDirect is currently the best option. It's still 2–4× the price of compounded tirzepatide, but it offers regulatory peace of mind.

Novo Nordisk launched a similar direct-to-consumer pilot for Wegovy in early 2025 — pricing is comparable but availability is more limited.

## Option 4: Insurance Appeal (If Marginal Coverage Exists)

If your insurance plan technically covers obesity drugs but denied your specific claim, an appeal is worth the effort. Successful appeals typically include:

1. Documented BMI ≥30 (or ≥27 with comorbidity)
2. Failed prior weight-loss attempts (commercial program records, prior medication trials)
3. Letter of medical necessity from your prescriber
4. Comorbidity documentation (sleep apnea, hypertension, prediabetes, etc.)

We maintain a state-by-state insurance coverage breakdown — see our [insurance checker tool](/insurance-checker) to see if your plan is one of the ~30% of commercial plans that cover Wegovy or Zepbound.

## What to Avoid

### International pharmacies

Canadian and UK pharmacies sell Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Zepbound at lower prices than U.S. retail. It is illegal for U.S. patients to import most of these medications for personal use, with very narrow exceptions. The FDA has explicitly warned against this practice. Aside from the legal risk, there is no way to verify cold-chain integrity for international shipping.

### "Research peptides" sold online

Numerous websites sell semaglutide and tirzepatide as "research peptides" or "not for human use" products. These are unregulated, frequently mislabeled or contaminated, and have caused multiple cases of serious adverse events. The FDA has issued warning letters to several of these vendors. Do not buy from these sources.

### Off-label Ozempic for weight loss

From 2021 to 2024, many patients without diabetes obtained off-label Ozempic prescriptions. In 2026, this is much harder: PBMs require lab documentation of diabetes for Ozempic claims, and the supply allocation has shifted to prioritize Wegovy for obesity indications. Trying to game the system is more trouble than it's worth.

## Putting It All Together

For most uninsured or under-insured patients in 2026, the decision tree is:

1. **Do you have commercial insurance (even if it doesn't cover obesity)?** Try the Wegovy or Zepbound savings card first.
2. **Are you on Medicare, Medicaid, or completely uninsured?** Choose between LillyDirect Zepbound vials (~$349–$599/mo, brand-name) and compounded semaglutide via telehealth (~$129–$249/mo).
3. **Want the lowest possible cost?** Compounded semaglutide via a verified telehealth provider, starting around $129/month.
4. **Want maximum regulatory certainty?** LillyDirect Zepbound vials.

Before committing to any path, plug your numbers into our [GLP-1 savings calculator](/savings-calculator) to see the apples-to-apples cost comparison for your specific situation. And read our [compounded semaglutide safety guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety) before signing up with any telehealth provider — knowing what to look for is the difference between a $149/month miracle and a $149/month problem.

For a deeper look at the brand-name landscape, see our [Ozempic vs Wegovy guide](/learn/glp-1-medications/ozempic-vs-wegovy).

body:
If you've read anything about compounded GLP-1 medications, you've probably encountered scary headlines: FDA warnings, hospitalizations, lawsuits, conflicting court decisions. You've also probably encountered glowing testimonials from patients paying $149/month instead of $1,349/month for semaglutide that produced the same weight loss. So which is true?

The honest answer is: both, depending entirely on which compounding pharmacy is producing the medication and which telehealth provider is dispensing it. Compounded semaglutide can be safe and effective, or it can be dangerous and even fraudulent. The difference comes down to a handful of regulatory and operational details that this guide will walk you through.

This is YMYL content. Every claim is sourced. We're not affiliated with any compounding pharmacy, and our [provider rankings](/best/semaglutide-providers) only include companies that meet the safety standards we'll define below.

## Key Takeaways

- **Compounded semaglutide is legal in 2026 under specific exemptions**, even though the parent drug is no longer on the FDA shortage list.
- **503B outsourcing facilities** are FDA-registered and inspected; they are the gold standard for compounded GLP-1 supply.
- **503A pharmacies** can compound for individual patients with documented medical need (e.g. allergies to inactive ingredients).
- **The biggest safety risks are not chemical** — they are sourcing fraud, dosing errors from non-standard concentrations, and lack of medical oversight.
- **Verify any provider** via [LegitScript](https://www.legitscript.com) and your state pharmacy board before paying.
- **Avoid:** "research peptides," Telegram/Reddit-only sellers, foreign pharmacies, providers with no licensed prescribers, and any source that ships without cold-chain packaging.

## What "Compounded" Actually Means

Drug compounding is the process of combining or altering ingredients to create a medication tailored to an individual patient's needs. It is a centuries-old pharmacy practice and is fully legal in the United States under federal and state law.

There are two types of compounding facilities:

### 503A pharmacies

Named after section 503A of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, these are state-licensed pharmacies that compound medications for individual patients pursuant to a valid prescription. They are regulated by state pharmacy boards (not the FDA directly). 503A pharmacies cannot mass-produce medications — each compound must be tied to a specific patient prescription.

### 503B outsourcing facilities

Named after section 503B of the same Act, these are FDA-registered facilities that can produce compounded medications in larger batches without a patient-specific prescription. They are inspected by the FDA, must comply with current Good Manufacturing Practices (cGMP), and report adverse events to FAERS (the FDA's adverse event database). 503B facilities are the gold standard for compounded supply at scale.

In the GLP-1 telehealth industry, the most reputable providers source from 503B outsourcing facilities. Mid-tier providers use 503A pharmacies. Bottom-tier and illegal providers source from unlicensed labs or import API from grey-market suppliers — this is where the safety problems concentrate.

## The FDA's Position: Complicated

From 2022 through October 2024, semaglutide was on the FDA drug shortage list. Federal law (under section 503A and 503B) explicitly permits compounding of drugs on the shortage list, even if those drugs are FDA-approved and commercially available, because the rationale is patient access.

In October 2024, the FDA declared the semaglutide shortage resolved. This triggered immediate legal challenges from compounding pharmacy trade associations (notably the Outsourcing Facilities Association and the Alliance for Pharmacy Compounding), who obtained court orders preventing the FDA from enforcing compounding restrictions during the litigation.

The legal and regulatory situation in 2026 is:

- **503B outsourcing facilities** can still produce compounded semaglutide under specific exemptions related to drug-specific patient need (e.g. patients who require a different concentration, patients who can't tolerate inactive ingredients in the brand product).
- **503A pharmacies** can compound semaglutide for individual patients with documented medical necessity.
- **The FDA has issued warnings** about specific bad actors (mislabeled products, inappropriate dosing, fraudulent providers) but has not banned compounded semaglutide as a category.

This is a moving target. The legal landscape may change again in 2026 or 2027. Reputable providers like the ones in our [best providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) closely track the regulatory situation and adjust sourcing accordingly.

## What Could Actually Go Wrong

When compounded semaglutide hurts patients, the cause is almost never the molecule itself. Semaglutide is a well-characterized peptide with decades of research behind it. The risks come from sourcing, manufacturing, and dispensing failures.

### 1. Mislabeled or contaminated API

The active pharmaceutical ingredient must be sourced from a licensed supplier with documented certificates of analysis. Grey-market API has been found to contain incorrect peptides, bacterial endotoxins, or wrong concentrations. 503B facilities are required to test every batch; sketchy compounders skip this.

### 2. Wrong concentration → dosing errors

Brand-name Wegovy comes in pre-filled pens that deliver exact doses (0.25 mg, 0.5 mg, 1.0 mg, 1.7 mg, 2.4 mg). Compounded semaglutide is typically supplied as a multi-dose vial in mg/mL concentration. If the patient or telehealth provider miscalculates units, an overdose is possible. Several FDA-reported adverse events traced to this exact mistake.

### 3. Loss of cold chain

Semaglutide requires refrigeration (36–46°F) for stability. Shipping without cold-chain packaging can degrade the peptide and reduce efficacy. Reputable providers ship in insulated boxes with ice packs and require signature on delivery.

### 4. "Semaglutide" that isn't semaglutide

Some bad actors sell salt forms (semaglutide sodium, semaglutide acetate) that are not the same as the FDA-approved semaglutide base. These salt forms are not approved for human use. The FDA has issued specific warnings against compounded products containing semaglutide salts.

### 5. No real medical oversight

Legitimate telehealth providers have licensed prescribers (MD, NP, PA) review every patient intake. Some bad-actor websites use rubber-stamp "medical reviews" or skip the prescriber step entirely. Without a real prescriber, there's no one to catch contraindications, drug interactions, or dose-titration errors.

## How to Verify a Provider Is Safe

Before signing up with any telehealth provider that offers compounded GLP-1, run through this checklist:

1. **LegitScript Healthcare Merchant Certification.** [LegitScript](https://www.legitscript.com) certifies telehealth providers and online pharmacies. Search the provider's domain on the LegitScript website. If they're not certified, walk away.
2. **Identifies the compounding pharmacy by name.** The provider's website should say which pharmacy fulfills their orders. Cross-reference that pharmacy with the FDA's [503B Outsourcing Facilities Registry](https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cder/registrationoutsourcing/) or your state pharmacy board's license database.
3. **Has licensed prescribers visible.** Reputable providers list their medical leadership (medical director, prescriber team) by name and credential. Anonymous "care team" with no names is a red flag.
4. **Cold-chain shipping with signature.** The provider should ship in insulated packaging with an ice pack and require a signature on delivery.
5. **Pre-filled syringes or clearly labeled vials.** The provider should ship medication in a format that minimizes dosing errors. Look for pre-measured doses or clearly labeled multi-dose vials with patient-specific instructions.
6. **Real pricing transparency.** Reputable providers list their pricing clearly. Sketchy ones bury the price behind a multi-step intake or quote different prices to different patients.
7. **Available in your state.** If a provider claims to ship to all 50 states but their compounding pharmacy is only licensed in 20, they're cutting regulatory corners.

All providers in our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) meet this checklist. We re-verify quarterly.

## Red Flags That Should Make You Walk Away

- The website says "research peptide" or "not for human use"
- Sold via Telegram, Discord, or Reddit DMs
- Ships from outside the United States
- No medical intake or prescriber review
- Won't name the compounding pharmacy
- No cold-chain packaging
- Price is dramatically lower than the market norm (e.g. $39/month — there's no legitimate way to produce compounded semaglutide that cheaply)
- No company address, no licensed prescribers listed, no LegitScript certification
- Ships in glass vials with handwritten labels or no labels at all

## Side Effects: Same as Brand-Name

When compounded semaglutide is produced correctly and sourced from a legitimate 503B facility, the side effect profile is identical to brand-name Ozempic and Wegovy: nausea, constipation, fatigue, occasional vomiting in the days after dose increases. Read our [semaglutide side effects management guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management) for the full clinical picture.

If you experience symptoms beyond the typical GI side effects — fever, severe abdominal pain, signs of infection at the injection site, sudden vision changes, severe allergic reactions — stop the medication immediately and contact a doctor. These can indicate either a serious adverse drug reaction OR a contamination event with the product itself.

## Bottom Line

Compounded semaglutide is safe when produced by a legitimate 503A or 503B pharmacy and dispensed by a licensed telehealth provider with real medical oversight. It is dangerous when sourced from grey-market vendors, foreign pharmacies, or unlicensed labs. The chemistry is the same; the operational integrity of the supply chain is what differs.

For most patients in 2026, the safest path to affordable GLP-1 access is a telehealth provider that:

1. Is LegitScript-certified
2. Sources from a named, FDA-registered 503B facility
3. Employs licensed prescribers who review every intake
4. Ships with cold-chain packaging and clear dosing instructions

Our [best GLP-1 providers ranking](/best/semaglutide-providers) is the shortlist of providers that meet these criteria. Our [methodology page](/methodology) explains how we audit each provider quarterly. And if you want to understand the broader cost landscape, see our guide on [getting GLP-1 without insurance](/learn/cost-and-insurance/glp-1-without-insurance).

body:
Semaglutide is one of the most effective weight loss medications ever developed — and also one of the most likely to make you feel terrible if you take too much, too fast. The dosing schedule is not arbitrary. It's specifically designed to let your body acclimate to the GLP-1 effects (slowed gastric emptying, suppressed appetite, altered gut motility) before you reach the doses that produce maximum weight loss.

This guide walks through the standard 16+ week titration schedule, what to expect at each step, when to slow down, and how to know when you've found your maintenance dose. It's based on the FDA-approved labeling for Wegovy and Ozempic plus the published evidence from the STEP clinical trial program. Your prescriber may modify this schedule based on your tolerance — and that's normal and often necessary.

For an interactive version of this schedule that adapts to your start date, use our [dose timeline tool](/dose-timeline). For the broader semaglutide picture, see our [semaglutide drug guide](/drugs/semaglutide).

## Key Takeaways

- **Standard titration is 16 weeks** to reach the 2.4 mg maintenance dose.
- **Each dose level lasts 4 weeks** before the next increase.
- **Side effects peak in the days after each dose increase** and usually subside within 2 weeks.
- **It is normal and acceptable to extend any dose level** if side effects are intolerable — patient comfort comes before reaching the target dose on schedule.
- **The 2.4 mg maintenance dose** is the dose tested in the pivotal STEP-1 trial that produced 14.9% mean weight loss.
- **Some patients respond well at lower doses** (1.0 mg or 1.7 mg) and never need to escalate to the maximum.

## The Standard Semaglutide Titration Schedule

Here is the FDA-approved dosing schedule for semaglutide, used by both Wegovy and Ozempic with minor variations:

| Week range | Dose | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Weeks 1–4 | 0.25 mg weekly | Initial — body acclimation |
| Weeks 5–8 | 0.5 mg weekly | First efficacy dose |
| Weeks 9–12 | 1.0 mg weekly | Building toward target |
| Weeks 13–16 | 1.7 mg weekly | Sub-maintenance (Wegovy only) |
| Week 17+ | 2.4 mg weekly | Maintenance (Wegovy target) |

For Ozempic (type 2 diabetes), the maintenance dose is typically 1.0 mg or 2.0 mg rather than 2.4 mg. For Wegovy (chronic weight management), 2.4 mg is the target.

See our [Ozempic vs Wegovy guide](/learn/glp-1-medications/ozempic-vs-wegovy) for why these two products use slightly different maintenance doses despite being the same active ingredient.

## Week-by-Week: What to Actually Expect

### Weeks 1–4: 0.25 mg (initial dose)

The 0.25 mg starting dose is intentionally below the threshold for meaningful weight loss. Its purpose is to let your gastrointestinal system acclimate to GLP-1 receptor activation without producing severe side effects.

**What to expect:**
- Mild nausea in the 24–72 hours after your first injection
- Slight reduction in appetite (you may notice you finish your meals less aggressively)
- Small changes in stool patterns (constipation or loose stools)
- Most patients feel essentially normal by the second injection

**Weight loss:** Typically 1–3 pounds total over the 4 weeks. Some patients lose nothing in this phase, which is normal.

**Tips:**
- Inject on the same day each week (e.g. every Sunday morning)
- Eat smaller, lower-fat meals on injection day and the day after
- Stay well hydrated

### Weeks 5–8: 0.5 mg (first efficacy dose)

At 0.5 mg, the appetite-suppressing effects become much more noticeable. Most patients see meaningful weight loss in this phase.

**What to expect:**
- Stronger appetite suppression — meals feel "complete" much earlier
- More noticeable nausea in the 24–48 hours after injection (especially the first 1–2 weeks at this dose)
- Possible constipation (this is common and worth addressing early)
- Some patients experience fatigue or brain fog

**Weight loss:** 1–2 pounds per week is typical. By the end of week 8, most patients have lost 5–10 pounds total since starting.

**When to slow down:**
- If nausea is severe enough to interfere with daily activities
- If you're vomiting more than once a week
- If you're losing weight too fast (>2.5 lb/week consistently for 3+ weeks)
- If you're not eating enough to meet protein and nutrient needs

### Weeks 9–12: 1.0 mg (building toward target)

The 1.0 mg dose is the standard maintenance dose for many type 2 diabetes patients on Ozempic. For weight loss patients, it's a stepping stone to higher doses, but some patients find it sufficient and never escalate further.

**What to expect:**
- Continued appetite suppression
- Side effects often *improve* compared to weeks 5–8 because your body has acclimated
- Some patients experience a renewed wave of nausea immediately after the dose increase, then improvement
- Weight loss continues at 1–2 lb/week for most

**Decision point:** Some patients hit their goal trajectory at 1.0 mg and don't need to escalate. Have an honest conversation with your prescriber at week 12.

### Weeks 13–16: 1.7 mg (sub-maintenance, Wegovy only)

This is a Wegovy-specific titration step — Ozempic does not include 1.7 mg as a standard dose. The purpose is to ease the transition to the 2.4 mg target.

**What to expect:**
- The dose increase often produces 3–7 days of renewed nausea
- Continued weight loss
- Most patients tolerate this dose well

**When to extend this dose:** If side effects are significant or you're meeting your weight loss goals at 1.7 mg, you can stay at this dose indefinitely. There is no requirement to escalate to 2.4 mg.

### Week 17+: 2.4 mg (Wegovy target maintenance)

The 2.4 mg dose is what was tested in STEP-1 and produced the headline 14.9% mean weight loss over 68 weeks. It is the target maintenance dose for Wegovy in patients who tolerate it.

**What to expect:**
- The strongest appetite suppression
- Maximum weight loss potential
- Side effects typically less severe than during titration because your body has fully acclimated
- Some patients still get GI symptoms in the days after each weekly injection

**How long to stay:** Indefinitely, as long as you tolerate it and continue to benefit. STEP-4 (a 68-week withdrawal trial) showed that patients who stopped semaglutide regained roughly two-thirds of the weight they had lost within a year. Maintenance is long-term.

## When to Slow Down or Hold a Dose

Your prescriber can — and should — modify the standard schedule based on your tolerance. Reasons to extend a dose level beyond 4 weeks:

- **Severe nausea** that interferes with hydration, nutrition, or daily activities
- **Frequent vomiting** (more than twice per week)
- **Significant constipation** that doesn't respond to fiber and water
- **Rapid weight loss** (>2.5 lb/week consistently)
- **Persistent fatigue** or inability to meet daily caloric needs
- **Hypoglycemia** in diabetes patients on insulin or sulfonylureas

Reasons to step back to a lower dose:

- **Persistent severe symptoms** at the current dose
- **New side effect** (e.g. gallbladder pain, severe abdominal pain) that requires evaluation
- **Pregnancy** (semaglutide is contraindicated in pregnancy and should be discontinued at least 2 months before conception)

A reputable prescriber will not push you to escalate on schedule if you're struggling. If you're working with a [GLP-1 telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers), the ability to easily message your prescriber and request a dose hold is one of the most important features to evaluate.

## Missed Dose Protocol

- **If your weekly dose is missed by less than 5 days:** take it as soon as you remember, then resume your normal schedule.
- **If your weekly dose is missed by more than 5 days:** skip the missed dose and take your next dose on the regularly scheduled day.
- **If you've missed 2+ doses (more than 14 days since your last injection):** contact your prescriber. They will likely have you re-titrate from a lower dose to avoid the GI symptoms that come with restarting at a high dose.

## Compounded Semaglutide Dosing

Compounded semaglutide from telehealth providers usually follows the same titration schedule as brand-name Wegovy. However, the exact dosing format varies — some providers ship pre-measured syringes, others ship multi-dose vials where you measure your own dose.

This is one of the most common sources of confusion (and dosing errors) in compounded semaglutide. Read our [compounded semaglutide safety guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety) for the specifics on what to look for in a provider's dosing protocol.

## Bottom Line

The semaglutide dosing schedule is a tool, not a mandate. The standard 4-weeks-per-step titration gets most patients to the 2.4 mg maintenance dose by week 17 with manageable side effects. But a sizeable minority of patients need to slow down, hold at a lower dose, or even drop back temporarily — and that's not failure, it's appropriate clinical management.

For an interactive version of this schedule that lets you input your start date and see your personalized week-by-week plan, use our [dose timeline tool](/dose-timeline). For complete information on the molecule itself, see our [semaglutide drug guide](/drugs/semaglutide). And for help managing the side effects that come with each dose increase, read our [semaglutide side effects management guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/semaglutide-side-effects-management).

title: Semaglutide Side Effects: Complete Guide to Managing Symptoms

body:
Semaglutide is highly effective for weight loss and type 2 diabetes — but as with any medication that produces meaningful biological change, it comes with side effects. Most are manageable. A few are serious. Understanding which is which — and what to do about each — is the difference between a frustrating treatment experience and a successful one.

This guide covers every notable semaglutide side effect, the clinical evidence behind it, and practical management strategies that work in real-world patient experience. It is YMYL content; every medical claim is sourced. If you're experiencing severe symptoms now and need urgent guidance, contact your prescriber or a 24/7 nurse line — this article is education, not medical advice.

For a broader picture of the medication itself, see our [semaglutide drug guide](/drugs/semaglutide). For dose-by-dose expectations, read our [semaglutide dosing schedule](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-dosing-schedule).

## Key Takeaways

- **80% of patients experience some GI side effect** at some point during titration; most are mild.
- **Nausea is the most common** and almost always improves within 2 weeks of each dose increase.
- **Constipation is more under-reported** than nausea but easier to manage with hydration and fiber.
- **Pancreatitis and gallbladder disease** are rare but serious — know the warning signs.
- **Slow titration is the single most effective prevention strategy** for almost every side effect.
- **Discontinuation due to side effects** in the STEP-1 trial was 4.5%, lower than most lifestyle interventions.

## The Most Common Side Effects (and How to Manage Them)

### Nausea

The single most common semaglutide side effect, reported by ~44% of patients in STEP-1. It usually peaks 24–72 hours after an injection — especially after a dose increase — and improves as your body acclimates over the following 1–2 weeks.

**Why it happens:** Semaglutide slows the rate at which your stomach empties. Food sits in the stomach longer, which produces a sensation of fullness that's adaptive for weight loss but can shade into nausea, especially after large or high-fat meals.

**Management:**
- Eat smaller, more frequent meals (4–6 small meals instead of 3 large ones)
- Avoid greasy, fried, or very high-fat foods on injection day and the day after
- Slow down — chewing thoroughly is more important on semaglutide than off it
- Stay hydrated; sip clear fluids throughout the day
- Ginger (real ginger, ginger tea, or ginger chews) is mildly effective
- If symptoms are severe, your prescriber can prescribe ondansetron (Zofran) or promethazine — common interventions in the GLP-1 telehealth space

**When to call your prescriber:** Nausea that prevents you from staying hydrated, vomiting more than twice a week, weight loss faster than 2.5 lb/week consistently, or any sign of dehydration (dark urine, dizziness, fatigue beyond normal).

### Constipation

Reported by ~24% of STEP-1 patients but probably under-reported. Slowed gastric emptying and reduced food intake combine to produce constipation in many patients.

**Management:**
- Increase water intake significantly (aim for 80+ oz per day)
- Add fiber gradually (psyllium husk, ground flaxseed, vegetables)
- Magnesium citrate (200–400 mg at bedtime) is well-tolerated and effective
- Walk or move daily — physical activity helps GI motility
- If constipation persists more than 5 days, discuss laxatives with your prescriber

### Vomiting

Reported by ~24% of patients in STEP-1. Usually associated with eating too much, eating too fast, or eating high-fat foods after a recent injection.

**Management:**
- Stop eating at the first sign of fullness (this takes practice — semaglutide changes how fullness feels)
- Avoid alcohol, especially in the first 48 hours after injection
- Avoid fried, fatty, or very spicy foods on injection day
- Sit upright for 30+ minutes after meals
- If vomiting is frequent (>2x/week), ask your prescriber to slow your titration or hold your current dose

### Diarrhea

Reported by ~30% of STEP-1 patients. Usually mild and self-limiting.

**Management:**
- Stay hydrated (electrolyte beverages, broth, oral rehydration solutions)
- Avoid dairy, caffeine, and high-sugar foods until symptoms resolve
- The BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) is gentle
- If diarrhea persists more than 3 days or is bloody, contact your prescriber

### Fatigue

Less discussed than GI symptoms but commonly reported by patients in real-world surveys. The cause is usually one of three things: significant calorie reduction (you're eating much less), dehydration, or low blood sugar (especially in patients also on insulin or sulfonylureas).

**Management:**
- Make sure you're hitting your protein target (0.8–1.0 g per pound of goal body weight)
- Stay hydrated
- If you're diabetic and on other glucose-lowering medications, discuss with your prescriber whether dosages need adjustment
- Sleep 7–9 hours per night
- Light exercise (walking) helps fatigue counterintuitively

### Headache

Reported by ~14% of STEP-1 patients. Often associated with dehydration or low blood sugar.

**Management:**
- Hydration first
- Eat regularly even when not hungry
- Standard OTC pain relievers (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) are fine for occasional use

## Less Common but Important Side Effects

### Injection site reactions

Mild redness, swelling, or itching at the injection site is common and resolves on its own. Rotate injection sites (abdomen, thigh, upper arm) and avoid injecting through clothing.

### Hair thinning

Some patients report hair shedding during rapid weight loss on semaglutide. This is usually due to telogen effluvium (a stress response to rapid weight loss, not a direct drug effect). It typically resolves within 6–12 months. Adequate protein and biotin intake may help.

### Mood changes

The FDA has investigated reports of suicidal ideation in GLP-1 patients but has not found a causal link. Still, if you experience new depression or anxiety while on semaglutide, discuss with your prescriber.

### Hypoglycemia

Low blood sugar is rare in non-diabetic patients on semaglutide. It's more common in diabetes patients also taking insulin or sulfonylureas, where dose adjustments may be needed.

## Serious (Rare) Side Effects — Know the Warning Signs

These are uncommon but require immediate medical attention.

### Pancreatitis

The FDA has flagged pancreatitis as a potential risk for all GLP-1 drugs, though large observational studies in 2023–2024 have shown the absolute risk is small. A 2024 JAMA Internal Medicine study put the incidence at roughly 0.1–0.2% in GLP-1 users, modestly elevated above baseline.

**Warning signs:**
- Severe abdominal pain that radiates to the back
- Pain that worsens after eating
- Nausea and vomiting that doesn't resolve
- Fever, rapid pulse

**What to do:** Stop the medication and seek immediate medical evaluation.

### Gallbladder disease

Rapid weight loss of any kind (not just GLP-1-induced) increases the risk of gallstones and gallbladder inflammation. STEP-1 reported gallbladder-related events in about 2.6% of semaglutide patients vs 1.2% of placebo over 68 weeks.

**Warning signs:**
- Sharp pain in the upper right abdomen, often after fatty meals
- Pain that radiates to the right shoulder or back
- Nausea, vomiting, fever
- Yellowing of skin or eyes (jaundice — this is urgent)

**What to do:** Contact your prescriber. Many patients can continue semaglutide after gallbladder evaluation; some require surgical intervention.

### Acute kidney injury

Severe dehydration from prolonged nausea and vomiting can cause acute kidney injury. This is preventable — stay hydrated and contact your prescriber if you can't keep fluids down.

### Thyroid C-cell tumors

Semaglutide carries a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors based on rodent studies. Human data has not confirmed this risk, but the drug is contraindicated in patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN2).

### Diabetic retinopathy complications

In patients with pre-existing diabetic retinopathy, rapid improvement in blood sugar from semaglutide can paradoxically worsen retinal disease in the short term. If you have diabetes and any history of eye disease, see an ophthalmologist before starting and during treatment.

## Side Effects by Dose

In STEP-1, the percentage of patients reporting each common side effect at the 2.4 mg maintenance dose:

| Side effect | Semaglutide 2.4 mg | Placebo |
|---|---|---|
| Nausea | 44.2% | 17.4% |
| Diarrhea | 31.5% | 15.9% |
| Vomiting | 24.8% | 6.6% |
| Constipation | 23.4% | 9.5% |
| Headache | 14.4% | 13.0% |
| Fatigue | 11.2% | 4.4% |
| Dyspepsia | 9.2% | 2.6% |

Most symptoms were rated as mild to moderate. Only 4.5% of semaglutide patients discontinued due to adverse events vs 0.8% on placebo.

## Slow Titration Is the Best Prevention

Every study and every clinician interview confirms the same finding: slow titration prevents most side effects. The standard 4-weeks-per-dose schedule exists for a reason. If you're struggling, ask your prescriber about extending each dose level to 6 or even 8 weeks. There is no medal for finishing titration on schedule.

Reputable [GLP-1 telehealth providers](/best/semaglutide-providers) make it easy to message your prescriber and request a dose hold or step-back. This is one of the most underrated features when comparing providers — see our [methodology](/methodology) for how we evaluate each provider's clinical responsiveness.

For a complete week-by-week guide to what to expect at each dose level, read our [semaglutide dosing schedule](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-dosing-schedule).

## When to Stop the Medication

Most side effects can be managed with patience, hydration, dietary adjustments, and dose modifications. But there are situations where stopping semaglutide is the right call:

- Confirmed pancreatitis
- Severe gallbladder disease requiring surgery
- Severe allergic reaction (rash, swelling, breathing difficulty)
- Pregnancy (semaglutide is contraindicated; discontinue at least 2 months before conception)
- Persistent inability to maintain adequate nutrition
- New diagnosis of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 in you or a first-degree relative

If you stop semaglutide, the appetite-suppressing effect wears off within a few weeks. STEP-4 showed that patients who stopped after a year regained roughly two-thirds of their lost weight within the following year. If you need to stop temporarily for a medical issue, talk to your prescriber about restarting at a lower dose to avoid re-experiencing initial titration symptoms.

## Bottom Line

Most semaglutide side effects are mild, predictable, and manageable. Nausea and constipation are the big two; both respond well to dietary adjustments, hydration, and dose pacing. The serious side effects (pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, kidney injury) are rare but real, and patients should know the warning signs.

The single most important thing you can do to minimize side effects is titrate slowly and communicate openly with your prescriber. If you're working with a [reputable telehealth provider](/best/semaglutide-providers), this is straightforward. If your provider doesn't make it easy to message a prescriber and request dose changes, that's a red flag — see our [compounded semaglutide safety guide](/learn/side-effects-and-safety/compounded-semaglutide-safety) for more on choosing a provider with real medical oversight.

For more on the underlying drug, see our [semaglutide drug guide](/drugs/semaglutide). For the head-to-head with tirzepatide (which has a similar but slightly different side effect profile), read [Semaglutide vs Tirzepatide](/learn/glp-1-medications/semaglutide-vs-tirzepatide).
